paper_title: Continual Unsupervised Domain Adaptation for Semantic Segmentation

problem_statement.challenge: Pixel-level labeling for real-world images requires high cost of time and labor
problem_statement.current_approach: Synthetic images can be used for automatic labeling but have domain discrepancy with real-world images
problem_statement.existing_solution: Unsupervised Domain Adaptation (UDA) techniques adapt networks trained on synthetic images to real images
problem_statement.limitation: Most existing UDA methods focus on single-target settings, which is impractical for real-world scenarios with multiple target domains introduced sequentially
problem_statement.issue: Blind application of existing UDA methods leads to catastrophic forgetting of previously learned target domains

proposed_solution.name: Expanding Target-specific Memory (ETM) framework

components.name: Target-specific Memory (TM)
components.description: A lightweight sub-memory for each target domain containing unique information corresponding to each domain discrepancy

components.name: Double Hinge Adversarial (DHA) loss
components.description: Enhances the performance of the framework

proposed_solution.mechanism: When testing on previous domains, the corresponding stored TM is used to overcome catastrophic forgetting

previous_work.method: Wu et al. [38] introduced a method for adapting to changing environments (weather, lighting)
previous_work.approach: Uses style-memory to transfer source environment style to target environments
previous_work.limitation: Experiments were conducted within one synthetic dataset, resulting in inferior performance when applied to multiple real-world datasets

figure_1.part_a: Shows traditional UDA method suffering from catastrophic forgetting in Continual UDA
figure_1.part_b: Shows ETM framework alleviating forgetting by expanding TM
figure_1.part_c: Shows qualitative results demonstrating ETM mitigates forgetting on previous target domain

title: Continual Unsupervised Domain Adaptation for Semantic Segmentation
